54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74

77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. 154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 87	\section{The morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from two global sections}
88	
89	\begin{lemma}
90	
91	\leanok
92	[Morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ from a globally generating pair of sections]
93	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
95	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
96	  Let $X$ be a scheme over a field $\bar k$, let $\mathcal L$ be an invertible
97	  sheaf on $X$, and let $s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)$ be two global
98	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
99	  \[
100	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
101	  \]
102	  (Equivalently, $s_0$ and $s_1$ generate $\mathcal L$ at every point.) Then
103	  there exists a unique morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
104	  \[
105	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
106	  \]
107	  whose pullback of $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)$ is $\mathcal L$ and whose
108	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
109	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))$ are $s_0$ and $s_1$ respectively. On the
110	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
111	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ the morphism $\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}$ is the map ``$x
112	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)$''; on the chart $D_+(x_1)$ it is the map ``$x
113	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
114	  $D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}$ by the cocycle identity
115	  $(s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1$.
116	\end{lemma}
117	
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
120	  \leanok
121	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
122	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to $\mathbb P^n$''), specialised to
123	  $n = 1$. Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
124	  under the name
125	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
126	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
127	
128	  \begin{quote}
129	  \small
130	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{$\sigma$ A\} [CommRing A]
131	    [SetLike $\sigma$ A] [AddSubgroupClass $\sigma$ A] ($\mathcal A$ :
132	    \(\mathbb N\) → $\sigma$) [GradedRing $\mathcal A$] \{X : Scheme\}\\
133	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj $\top$)\\
134	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant $\mathcal A$).toIdeal = $\top$) :\\
135	    \phantom{}\quad(X $\longrightarrow$ Proj $\mathcal A$)}
136	  \end{quote}
137	
138	  Specialising to $\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ with the standard $\mathbb
139	  N$-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded $\bar k$-algebra
140	  homomorphism $f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ and a
141	  proof that the irrelevant ideal $(x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]$ maps to
142	  the unit ideal of $H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)$ (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
143	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
144	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of $\mathbb
145	  P^n$ as a $\mathrm{Proj}$). When $\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
146	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
147	  cover $X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)$, where on each chart the sections become
148	  honest global functions ($s_1 / s_0$ on $D(s_0)$, $s_0 / s_1$ on $D(s_1)$,
149	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
150	  project-bespoke wrapper
151	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
152	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
153	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
154	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
155	
156	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
157	  a separated scheme: two morphisms $\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1$
158	  with $\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i$ for $i = 0, 1$ agree on the basic-open
159	  charts $D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)$ (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
160	  the same rational function), and these charts cover $\mathbb P^1$, so the
161	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
162	\end{proof}

164	\section{Degree of a morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via its pole divisor}
165	
166	\begin{lemma}
167	
168	\leanok
169	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via the preimage of $\infty$]
170	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
172	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point}
173	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
174	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
175	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
176	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
177	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
178	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
179	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
180	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ be a non-constant morphism
181	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$. Then
182	  $\varphi$ is finite, its function-field extension
183	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ is finite, and the degree of
184	  $\varphi$
185	  \[
186	    \deg(\varphi) \;:=\; [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] \;\in\; \mathbb N_{>0}
187	  \]
188	  is recovered as the degree of the pullback of any closed point of $\mathbb
189	  P^1$: for every closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$,
190	  \[
191	    \deg(\varphi^*[Q]) \;=\; \deg(\varphi).
192	  \]
193	  In particular, taking $Q = \infty \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the morphism
194	  satisfies
195	  \[
196	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\;
197	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
198	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
199	  \]
200	  where $t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)$ is a local parameter at $\infty$
201	  (equivalently $t_\infty = 1/u$ for the standard affine coordinate $u$ on
202	  $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$).
203	\end{lemma}
204	
205	\begin{proof}
206	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point,
207	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
208	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$ is
209	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of $\varphi$ is a closed
210	  irreducible subset of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ that is not a point (else
211	  $\varphi$ would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
212	  P^1_{\bar k}$ (the only proper irreducible closed subset of $\mathbb
213	  P^1_{\bar k}$ is a point); hence $\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
214	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
215	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
216	  fact that $\varphi$ has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
217	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
218	  contradicting irreducibility of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $C$ at
219	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
220	  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$
221	  is finite.
222	
223	  The identification $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$ for
224	  any closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$ is the multiplicativity of the
225	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
226	  $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
227	  \Div(C')$, $\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
228	  to $D = [Q]$ (a divisor of degree $1$ since $\kappa(Q) = \bar k$) gives
229	  $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)$. Equivalently, the degree of
230	  $\varphi$ equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
231	  P^1$, counted with the ramification multiplicities $\ord_P(\varphi^*
232	  t_\infty)$ at the preimage points $P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
233	
234	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
235	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
236	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
237	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
238	  and the multiplicativity of $\deg$ under composition of $\div$ and
239	  $\deg$; the closely related ``$\div(f)$ has degree zero'' specialisation
240	  is \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
241	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
242	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
243	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
244	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
245	\end{proof}
246	
247	\section{A degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}
250	
251	\leanok
252	[Degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
253	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
255	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
256	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
257	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
258	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
259	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
260	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
261	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
262	  % equivalent:}
263	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
264	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
265	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over $k$, and $k$-homomorphisms.}"
266	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
267	  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
268	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
269	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
270	  (p.~299).}
271	  Let $C, C'$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
272	  algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ be
273	  a non-constant morphism with $\deg(\varphi) = 1$. Then $\varphi$ is an
274	  isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	  By \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} (specialised to a non-constant
279	  morphism, not necessarily to $\mathbb P^1$; the same argument applies to
280	  any non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$),
281	  the morphism $\varphi$ is finite and the function-field extension
282	  $\varphi^\# \colon \bar k(C') \hookrightarrow \bar k(C)$ has degree
283	  $[\bar k(C) : \bar k(C')] = \deg(\varphi) = 1$. Therefore $\varphi^\#$
284	  is an isomorphism of function fields $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$.
285	
286	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment $C \mapsto \bar k(C)$ is
287	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
288	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
289	  one over $\bar k$ (with $\bar k$-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
290	  the isomorphism $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$ produced by the previous
291	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
292	  C'$, and this isomorphism agrees with $\varphi$ on the generic point (by
293	  construction: it is the inverse-image of $\varphi^\#$ under the
294	  equivalence). The two morphisms $C \to C'$ that agree on the generic
295	  point of the integral scheme $C$ agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
296	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
297	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence $\varphi$ is itself
298	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
299	
300	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
301	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
302	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-$1$ morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
303	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
304	  Indeed, the direct image $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C$ is a coherent
305	  $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
306	  1$, and an inclusion $\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
307	  \mathcal O_C$ of coherent rank-$1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
308	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
309	  $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}$ has trivial stalk. The
310	  quotient is a torsion coherent $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module of generic rank
311	  $\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0$, hence is itself torsion-free (a
312	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
313	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
314	  \mathcal O_{C'}$ and $\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
315	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
316	
317	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
318	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
319	  non-constant finite morphism $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ together with the
320	  degree-$1$ hypothesis $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ and produces an isomorphism
321	  $C \cong C'$ in the slice category $\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}$.
322	  The proof body threads the Mathlib API
323	  \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
324	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
325	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
326	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
327	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
328	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
329	\end{proof}

331	\section{Headline: genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
332	
333	\begin{theorem}
334	
335	\leanok
336	[Genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$]
337	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
339	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
340	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
341	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
342	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
343	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
344	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S1 Example~1.3.5 (genus-zero classification;
345	  % "rational $\iff g = 0$" + "rational curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ for
346	  % complete nonsingular curves") (read from
347	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314, doc p.~297).
348	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
349	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this
350	  % chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve $X$ is
351	  % rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now using
352	  % (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We
353	  % already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
354	  % suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two distinct
355	  % points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor $D = P - Q$.
356	  % Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$,
357	  % and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by
358	  % (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies
359	  % $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
360	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
361	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, and let $C$ be a smooth
362	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$.
363	  Then there exists an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes
364	  \[
365	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
366	  \]
367	  Concretely, the Lean signature
368	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
369	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
370	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
371	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
372	  \Cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} is its consumer-side restatement,
373	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
374	\end{theorem}
375	
376	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
377	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
378	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
379	% "We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots"). The present
380	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
381	% (linear-system construction $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
382	% ([P]))$, degree-$1$ identification, isomorphism).
383	\begin{proof}
384	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
385	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
386	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
387	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
388	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
389	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ the smooth proper
390	  geometrically irreducible curve $C$ has closed points, all of which are
391	  $\bar k$-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
392	  $\bar k$ is a finite extension of $\bar k$, hence is $\bar k$ itself by
393	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such $\bar k$-rational point $P \in C$;
394	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
395	
396	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$.}
397	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
398	  (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
399	  effective divisor $[P]$ of degree $1$, we obtain
400	  $\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2$. The constant section
401	  $1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ accounts for a
402	  one-dimensional $\bar k$-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
403	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
404	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
405	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
406	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
407	
408	  \emph{Step 2: morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via the linear system $(1, f)$.}
409	  The pair $(1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2$ generates the
410	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ at every point of $C$: at points
411	  $Q \in C \setminus \{P\}$ the section $1$ does not vanish in
412	  $\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}$, and at the marked point
413	  $P$ the section $f$ does not vanish in $\mathcal O_C([P])_P$ (because $f$
414	  has a pole of order exactly $1$ at $P$ in the rational-function
415	  picture: $f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k$ with $\div(f)_\infty = [P]$).
416	  Therefore by \Cref{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
417	  exists a unique morphism
418	  \[
419	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
420	  \]
421	  with $\varphi^* x_0 = 1$ and $\varphi^* x_1 = f$ (so the pullback of
422	  the standard ratio coordinate $u = x_1 / x_0$ on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
423	  \{\infty\}$ is $f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
424	  $t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f$ is the local parameter $1/f$ at $P$).
425	
426	  \emph{Step 3: $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ via the pole divisor of $f$.} The
427	  morphism $\varphi$ is non-constant (since $f$ is non-constant as a
428	  rational function on $C$, the pullback $\varphi^* u = f$ takes
429	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant $\varphi$). By
430	  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to $\varphi$,
431	  \[
432	    \deg(\varphi)
433	    \;=\;
434	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
435	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
436	  \]
437	  We compute the pole divisor of $f$ as a rational function on $C$:
438	  since $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ with $f$ non-constant and
439	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ the line bundle of the effective divisor $[P]$, the
440	  rational-function $f$ has pole divisor $(f)_\infty = [P]$ (the
441	  unique pole of order $1$ at $P$, no other poles; this is the standard
442	  identification of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$ with the rational functions
443	  $g \in K(C)$ satisfying $\div(g) + D \geq 0$, specialised to
444	  $D = [P]$). Hence $\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}$ with multiplicity~$1$
445	  and
446	  \[
447	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
448	  \]
449	
450	  \emph{Step 4: $\varphi$ is an isomorphism.} By
451	  \Cref{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant degree-$1$
452	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (smooth proper
453	  geometrically irreducible curves over $\bar k$ on both sides), $\varphi$
454	  is an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
455	
456	  This produces the required isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$
457	  closing the headline target
458	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
459	
460	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
461	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
462	  $P, Q \in C$ and the degree-zero divisor $D = P - Q$, deriving $P \sim Q$
463	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that $C$ is rational; combined with I.6.12
464	  this yields $C \cong \mathbb P^1$. The present proof restates the same
465	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
466	  construction: instead of going through the $P \sim Q$ statement and
467	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
468	  $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1$ from the two sections $(1, f)$ of
469	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
470	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
471	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero $\Rightarrow$
472	  $\ell([P]) = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ non-constant $f$ $\Rightarrow$ degree-$1$
473	  map to $\mathbb P^1$ $\Rightarrow$ iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
474	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
475	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
476	\end{proof}

4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.

99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	
104	\leanok
105	[Line bundle of a closed point]
106	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
108	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
109	        def:order_at_point}
110	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
111	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
112	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
113	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
114	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
115	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
116	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
117	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
118	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
119	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
120	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
121	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let $X$ be a scheme. Then:
122	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor $D$, $\mathscr L(D)$ is an invertible
123	  % sheaf on $X$.}"
124	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
125	  $\mathscr L(D)$ and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
126	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
127	  closed point. Let $\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}$ denote the
128	  maximal ideal of the local ring at $P$; by smoothness of $C$,
129	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation ring and $\mathfrak m_P$
130	  is generated by any uniformizer $f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$ (an
131	  element of $\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2$). The Cartier
132	  divisor associated to the closed point $P$ is the effective
133	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer $f_P$ on an open
134	  affine neighbourhood of $P$, and by the unit $1$ on the complement
135	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. The \emph{line bundle of the closed point $P$},
136	  denoted $\mathcal O_C(P)$, is the locally free $\mathcal O_C$-module
137	  of rank $1$ defined by Hartshorne's construction $\mathscr L(D)$
138	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
139	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
140	  $\mathcal K_C$ (the constant sheaf of the function field $K(C)$ on the
141	  integral scheme $C$) that is generated by $f_P^{-1} \in K(C)$ on a
142	  neighbourhood of $P$ and by $1 \in K(C)$ on the complement
143	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
144	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
145	  uniformizer $f_P$ (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
146	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}$, so they generate the same
147	  $\mathcal O_C$-submodule of $\mathcal K_C$).
148	
149	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
150	  $\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C$ denote the ideal sheaf of the
151	  closed point $P$ (locally generated by the uniformizer $f_P$ near
152	  $P$, and equal to $\mathcal O_C$ on the complement). Then
153	  $\mathcal I_P$ is the line bundle $\mathcal O_C(-P)$ associated to the
154	  Weil divisor $-[P]$, and $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
155	  $\mathcal O_C$-linear dual:
156	  \[
157	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
158	    \mathcal O_C)
159	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
160	  \]
161	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
162	  ``subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$'' description above is the one used in
163	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
164	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
165	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
166	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
167	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
168	
169	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
171	  scheme $C$, a point $P : C$, and a closedness witness
172	  $\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}$ (the same input shape as
173	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), and returns an invertible
174	  $\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P)$. The well-definedness as an
175	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
176	  smoothness of $C$ (delivering the DVR stalk at $P$) and the standard
177	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
178	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
179	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
180	  closed plus dimension-$1$-integral delivers $\codim_C \{P\} = 1$
181	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
182	\end{definition}

184	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
185	
186	\begin{lemma}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
191	controlled pole]
192	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
194	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
195	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
196	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
197	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
198	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
199	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
200	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
201	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
203	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
204	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$
205	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
206	  % (a) for each nonzero $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$, the divisor of
207	  % zeros $(s)_0$ is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$;
208	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$ is $(s)_0$
209	  % for some $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$; and
210	  % (c) two sections $s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$ have the same
211	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a $\lambda \in k^*$ such
212	  % that $s' = \lambda s$.}"
213	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If $D \geq 0$ and $D = D_0 + (f)$, then
214	  % $(f) \geq -D_0$. Thus $f$ gives a global section of
215	  % $\mathscr L(D_0)$ whose divisor of zeros is $D$."
216	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
217	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
218	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, let $P \in C$ be a
219	  closed point, and let $\mathcal O_C(P)$ be the line bundle of
220	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. The $\bar k$-vector space of
221	  global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is naturally identified with the
222	  Riemann--Roch space
223	  \[
224	    L([P]) \;:=\;
225	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
226	    \text{ in } \Div(C)\bigr\}
227	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
228	  \]
229	  Equivalently, the order conditions
230	  \[
231	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
232	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
233	    \qquad
234	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
235	  \]
236	  on $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ are equivalent to the existence of a global
237	  section $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ whose image $\iota(s)$ under
238	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
239	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ equals $f$ (the iff binds $s$ to $f$
240	  explicitly — vacuous-in-$f$ readings such as ``some nonzero global
241	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
242	
243	  Under this identification, the inclusion
244	  $\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of constant functions
245	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
246	  \mathcal O_C(P)$ exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
247	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor $[P]$.
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
252	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
253	  By construction, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
254	  the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathcal K_C$ generated locally by
255	  $f_P^{-1}$ near $P$ and by $1$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$, where $f_P$ is
256	  a uniformizer at $P$ (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}). Since $C$
257	  is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the constant sheaf at the function field
258	  $K(C)$, and a global section of any sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module
259	  $\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C$ is precisely an element of $K(C)$
260	  whose germ at every point lies in $\mathcal F$ at that point.
261	  Therefore $f \in K(C)$ is a global section of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if
262	  and only if:
263	
264	  \begin{itemize}
265	    \item For every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$, the germ of
266	      $f$ at $Q$ lies in $\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}$
267	      (the structure sheaf, since $\mathcal O_C(P)$ agrees with
268	      $\mathcal O_C$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$); equivalently,
269	      $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$.
270	    \item At the closed point $P$, the germ of $f$ at $P$ lies in
271	      $\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}$;
272	      equivalently, $f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$, i.e.\
273	      $\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$.
274	  \end{itemize}
275	  In divisorial language (\Cref{def:principal_divisor},
276	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), the two conditions combine to
277	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
278	  $L([P])$. The zero element of $K(C)$ is included by convention as the
279	  zero global section.
280	
281	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
282	  taking $D_0 = [P]$ effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
283	  rational function $f$ with $(f) \geq -D_0$ defines a global section
284	  of $\mathscr L(D_0)$. The condition $(f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]$ is exactly
285	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$. The compatibility with the constant inclusion
286	  follows by taking $f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}$: then $\div(1) = 0$, so
287	  $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, and the corresponding section of
288	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
289	  \cong \bar k$ under the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C
290	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
291	\end{proof}

293	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
294	
295	\begin{lemma}
296	
297	
298	\leanok
299	[Vanishing of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
300	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
302	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
303	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
304	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
305	  % step ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point.''):
306	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
307	  % k(P) \to 0$ and its tensor with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
308	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$
309	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
311	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
312	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
313	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
314	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
315	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
316	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
317	  % (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
318	  % does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$)."
319	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
320	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
321	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
322	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
323	  \[
324	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
325	  \]
326	  i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{proof}
330	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
331	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
332	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at $D = 0$. The standard short
333	  exact sequence
334	  \[
335	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
336	  \]
337	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
338	  subscheme $P$ is $\mathcal O_C(-[P])$; the quotient is the structure
339	  sheaf of $P$, which is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$
340	  supported at $P$ since $P$ has residue field $\bar k$ by closedness
341	  over the algebraically closed base $\bar k$). Tensoring with the
342	  locally free rank-$1$ sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$
343	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}) preserves exactness and leaves
344	  the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged, giving
345	  \[
346	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
347	  \]
348	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$. Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
349	  cohomology (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, the project's $\Module \bar
350	  k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that $\dim C = 1$ kills
351	  $H^i$ for $i \geq 2$ (Grothendieck vanishing on a $1$-dimensional
352	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
353	  \begin{multline*}
354	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
355	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
356	  \end{multline*}
357	  Two ingredients close the proof:
358	
359	  \emph{(a) $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$.} This is the genus-$0$
360	  hypothesis: by \Cref{def:genus},
361	  \[
362	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
363	  \]
364	  so $g(C) = 0$ is exactly $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$,
365	  i.e.\ $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
366	
367	  \emph{(b) $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.} The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ is
368	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
369	  on an open $U \subset C$ either restricts to $0$ (if $P \notin U$) or
370	  takes values in the stalk $k(P)_P \cong \bar k$ (if $P \in U$), and
371	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
372	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
373	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
374	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$, and \emph{a fortiori}
375	  $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.
376	
377	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
378	  segment ending at $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to
379	  \[
380	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
381	  \]
382	  whence $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$, as required.
383	
384	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
385	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
386	  $\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)$ at the abelian-group level
387	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
388	  project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline
389	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) inherits the long exact sequence by
390	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
391	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$ is closed by either the flasque
392	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
393	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
394	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
395	  $C \setminus \{P\}$ and a small affine open around $P$ (the
396	  skyscraper has zero sections on $C \setminus \{P\}$, so the Čech
397	  complex degenerates).
398	\end{proof}

401	
402	\begin{theorem}
403	
404	
405	\leanok

411	        def:eulerChar_curve,

413	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}

415	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with $D = [P]$: $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
416	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
417	  % $H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0$ (read from
418	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
419	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
420	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
421	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
422	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
423	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
424	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
425	  % is the Euler characteristic
426	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
427	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
428	  $\chi$-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
429	  genus-$0$ specialisation chain).}

431	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
432	  \[

434	  \]

439	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,

444	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of \texttt{RR.2} to

449	  \[

451	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.

489	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in

120	
121	- **Mathlib idiom**: when stating "morphism induces iso on function
122	  fields", Mathlib's convention is to state things in terms of
123	  `Scheme.functionField` as a `CommRingCat` (it's the *literal* field
124	  at the generic point, not a `Field` type-synonym). The induced
125	  function-field map for a `φ : X ⟶ Y` of integral schemes would be the
126	  composite
127	  `Y.functionField → Y.presheaf.stalk (φ.base ξ_X) →[stalkMap]→
128	   X.presheaf.stalk ξ_X → X.functionField`
129	  using `germToFunctionField` + `IsFractionRing.lift`. There is no
130	  named `Scheme.Hom.functionFieldMap` in Mathlib — this is a small
131	  derived definition the project would write inline.

133	- **Project's current path**: the lemma takes an *abstract* ring iso
134	  `Nonempty (C'.left.functionField ≃+* C.left.functionField)` as a
135	  hypothesis, NOT specifically the iso induced by `φ`. This is a
136	  deliberate weakening (per the docstring L334-341): "the existence
137	  wrapper matches the file-skeleton's substantive-but-non-tautological-
138	  type discipline without committing prematurely to a specific Mathlib
139	  formulation of the canonical function-field map."
140	
141	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost (mild). The hypothesis is *strictly
142	  weaker* than needed for the iter-178+ body to extract a useful
143	  birational map: any ring iso `K(C') ≃+* K(C)` will do, but the
144	  birational-extension argument needs the iso induced by `φ` itself.
145	  Concretely, the existence hypothesis tells you nothing about how
146	  the abstract field iso relates to `φ`'s action on the generic point.
147	
148	- **Cost of divergence**: medium. The iter-178+ body will need to
149	  *strengthen* the hypothesis (or replace it with a non-constancy +
150	  degree-from-field-extension formulation) before the
151	  normalisation-based argument can proceed.
152	
153	- **Verdict**: **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY**, but flag for re-statement
154	  during iter-178+ body work. The current existence hypothesis is a
155	  file-skeleton placeholder; the planner should anticipate refining

163	Both decisions land **PROCEED**: Mathlib has no

\begin{definition}
[Inclusion of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ into the function field]
  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of $\mathscr L(D)$ as a
  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$; the
  % canonical inclusion $\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K$ is part
  % of the construction) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of $\mathscr L(D)$
  as a subsheaf of $\mathscr K$).}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$
  with $C$ integral, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. The line bundle
  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ of \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} is by
  construction a sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient
  rings $\mathcal K_C$. Since $C$ is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the
  constant sheaf at the function field $K(C)$, and taking global sections
  of the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C$
  produces a canonical $\bar k$-linear map
  \[
    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
  \]
  sending a global section $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to the rational
  function on $C$ obtained by composing $s$ with the inclusion of
  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ into $\mathcal K_C$ at each point. The map $\iota$ is
  injective (the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
  \mathcal K_C$ is torsion-free of rank $1$, so taking global sections
  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme $C$).

  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
  takes the same inputs as \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} (a curve
  $C$, a point $P$, and the closedness witness
  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
  section $s$ of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ at twist $0$ in the project's
  $\Module \bar k$-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
  element $\iota(s) \in K(C)$ of the project's function-field carrier
  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
  and its body is gated on the body of
  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}.
\end{definition}

\section{A degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}

% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to $\varphi$) was strictly
% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
% only the input shape changes.
\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Degree-$1$ morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
  % equivalence of categories) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
  % equivalent:}
  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over $k$, and $k$-homomorphisms.}"
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
  (p.~299).}
  Let $C, C'$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
  algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ be
  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
  degree one,
  \[
    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
  \]
  formally
  $\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1$ in the Lean signature.
  Then $\varphi$ is an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.

  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
  lemma, the $K(C')$-algebra structure on $K(C)$ used to interpret the
  degree $[K(C) : K(C')]$ is the canonical $\varphi$-induced
  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
  \[
    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
  \]
  of the germ at the image of the generic point $\xi_C \in C$ with the
  stalk-map of $\varphi$ at $\xi_C$ and the fraction-field lift at
  $\xi_C$ (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  By \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} (specialised to a non-constant
  morphism, not necessarily to $\mathbb P^1$; the same argument applies to
  any non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over $\bar k$),
  the morphism $\varphi$ is finite and the function-field extension
  $\varphi^\# \colon \bar k(C') \hookrightarrow \bar k(C)$ has degree
  $[\bar k(C) : \bar k(C')] = \deg(\varphi) = 1$. Therefore $\varphi^\#$
  is an isomorphism of function fields $\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)$.
input.new_string:
\begin{proof}
  The function-field hypothesis $[K(C) : K(C')] = 1$ states that the
  canonical $\varphi$-induced map $\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)$ is a
  finite extension of degree one; since $K(C')$ is a field and the
  extension has dimension one as a $K(C')$-vector space, $\varphi^\#$ is
  an isomorphism of function fields $K(C') \cong K(C)$. (Equivalently, by
  the IV.2-opening identification $\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]$, this is
  the statement $\deg(\varphi) = 1$; the morphism $\varphi$ is finite in
  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)

\section{Degree of a morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via its pole divisor}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Degree of a non-constant morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via the preimage of $\infty$]
  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
  Let $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ be a non-constant morphism
  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$. Then
  $\varphi$ is finite, its function-field extension
  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ is finite, and the degree of
  $\varphi$
  \[
    \deg(\varphi) \;:=\; [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] \;\in\; \mathbb N_{>0}
  \]
  is recovered as the degree of the pullback of any closed point of $\mathbb
  P^1$: for every closed point $Q \in \mathbb P^1$,
  \[
    \deg(\varphi^*[Q]) \;=\; \deg(\varphi).
  \]
  In particular, taking $Q = \infty \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the morphism
  satisfies
  \[
    \deg(\varphi) \;=\;
    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
  \]
  where $t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)$ is a local parameter at $\infty$
  (equivalently $t_\infty = 1/u$ for the standard affine coordinate $u$ on
  $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$).
\end{lemma}

\section{Degree of a morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via its pole divisor}

% NOTE (iter-182 plan-phase Pin 2 signature strengthening). The iter-177
% file-skeleton placeholder existential
%   "$\exists (d : \mathbb N) (D : C.\mathrm{WeilDivisor}),\
%      0 < d \wedge D.\mathrm{degree} = d$"
% was vacuous in $\varphi$ (no constraint tying $D$ to $\varphi$ or the
% point $\infty$). The iter-182 plan-phase refactor (refactor slug
% `pin2-sig-strengthen`) replaces it with the substantive existential
% pinning $D$ to $\varphi^*[\infty]$ and the degree to the function-field
% extension degree $[K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$. The new typed-sorry def
% `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` (in
% `RationalCurveIso.lean`, analogous to the iter-181 typed-sorry def
% `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` of OCofP.lean) carries the
% Weil divisor $\varphi^*[\infty]$; its body is queued for iter-183+
% via the affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route.
\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Degree of a non-constant morphism to $\mathbb P^1$ via the preimage of $\infty$]
  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:order_at_point, def:divisor_closed_point}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
  % $f: X \to Y$ of curves is defined to be the degree $[K(X):K(Y)]$ of the
  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
  Let $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ be a non-constant morphism
  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$. Then
  $\varphi$ is finite, its function-field extension
  $\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ (the canonical
  $\varphi$-induced map, supplied as an
  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
  divisor $D \in \Div(C)$ such that
  \[
    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
    \quad\text{and}\quad
    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
  \]
  Equivalently, the morphism $\varphi$ satisfies
  \[
    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
  \]
  where $t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)$ is a local parameter at $\infty$
  (equivalently $t_\infty = 1/u$ for the standard affine coordinate $u$ on
  $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$); and for any closed point
  $Q \in \mathbb P^1$ the same identification gives
  $\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]$.

  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
  $\varphi^*[\infty]$ is exposed in Lean as
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor $\varphi$}, a
  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point $P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$
  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
  $\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)$ from the maximal ideal at $\infty$ in
  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$). On the Lean side, the existential output of
  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
  $D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi$ together with
  $\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)$,
  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
  upstream of its consumer lemma.
\end{lemma}

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
ratcurveiso-pin3-prose

## Status
COMPLETE

## Target chapters
- `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex`
- `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex`

(Two chapters edited per directive — Pin 3 + Pin 2 signature refresh
on the RR.4 chapter, and a new typed-sorry-def declaration block on
the RR.3 chapter.)

## Changes Made

### `RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex`

- **Revised** `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` (statement block) —
  replaced the implicit `\deg(\varphi) = 1` hypothesis with the
  explicit function-field-extension-degree-one formulation
  `[K(C) : K(C')] = 1`, formally
  `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1`. Added a
  "Convention on the function-field map" paragraph documenting the
  canonical `[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]` instance
  threaded into the Lean signature at each call-site (composite of
  `Scheme.Hom.stalkMap` at the generic point with
  `IsFractionRing.lift`), per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`
  Decision 2.
  - Added a leading `% NOTE: ...` LaTeX comment referencing
    iter-181 Lane I + the analogies file with verdict
    `DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY`.
  - The proof prose body (Hartshorne I.6.12 + alternative
    scheme-theoretic argument) is mathematically unchanged; only
    the proof's opening sentence was rephrased to match the new
    hypothesis shape (and the closing `Sub-build note.` paragraph
    was refreshed to mention the `[Algebra ...]` instance and the
    `Module.finrank ... = 1` hypothesis).
- **Revised** `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` (statement block) —
  refactored the placeholder existential
  `∃ d D, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = d` (vacuous in `φ`) into the
  substantive existential
  `∃ D, D = φ^*[∞] ∧ deg(D) = [K(C):k̄(P¹)]`. Added a "Convention
  on the pole divisor" paragraph documenting the typed-sorry def
  `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` and the iter-183+ closure route via
  `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`. Added a leading `% NOTE: ...`
  LaTeX comment referencing the iter-182 plan-phase refactor slug
  `pin2-sig-strengthen`.
- **Revised** `thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1` Step 4 — added a one-line
  bridge that re-expresses the Step 3 conclusion
  `\deg(\varphi) = 1` as
  `Module.finrank k̄(P¹) K(C) = 1` (via Hartshorne IV.2) so the
  invocation of `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` matches its new
  function-field-extension-degree hypothesis shape.

### `RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex`

- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}`/
  `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}`
  — informal statement of the canonical $\bar k$-linear inclusion
  $\iota : H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \hookrightarrow K(C)$ obtained by
  composing with the structural inclusion
  $\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C$ at each point.
  Placed after the existing `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint` block (and
  before `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff`) per the
  directive. Citation block reuses the same II.6 source quote (the
  $\mathscr L(D)$-as-subsheaf-of-$\mathscr K$ definition) already
  used by `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint`.
- **Revised** `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff`'s
  `\uses{...}` line — added
  `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField` so the dependency
  graph picks up the new node.

## Cross-references introduced

- `\uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}` added to
  `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff` (statement block;
  the proof's `\uses{...}` was left unchanged because the proof
  derives the inclusion from the parent definition directly without
  invoking the named map). Both blocks live in the same chapter, so
  the cross-reference resolves locally.
- `\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}` is referenced
  from the new
  `Pin 2 ... \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}` paragraph in
  `RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex` (as a cross-chapter pattern
  pointer — "mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
  is introduced upstream of its consumer lemma"). The cross-chapter
  `\Cref` resolves via `leanblueprint`'s global label scope.

## References consulted

- `references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.md` — registry / source
  card for Hartshorne. Confirmed the local file path
  `references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf` and verified the
  II.6 verbatim source quote (the
  `\mathscr L(D)`-as-subsheaf-of-`\mathscr K` definition, PDF
  page 161) was already character-by-character extracted in the
  existing `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint` and is re-used (identical
  bytes) for the new `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField`
  block.
- `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` — Decision 2 (the
  `DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY` verdict that drove the iter-181 Lane I
  signature refinement to `Module.finrank ... = 1` with a
  `[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]` instance argument).
  Used to back the `% NOTE: ...` comment in `Edit 1`.

## Macros needed (if any)
None. All new prose uses existing macros (`\Cref`, `\Div`, `\div`,
`\ord`, `\deg`, `\mathcal O_C(P)`, `\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}`,
`\bar k`, `\texttt{...}`). No new commands introduced.

## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
None. The directive's "may need a child reference-retriever dispatch"
was conditional on `ls references/hartshorne*` being empty; that
check returned a populated entry (`hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.md`
+ `.pdf` present, both already verified by prior iters with the same
verbatim quote in-tree), so no retrieval was needed.

## Notes for Plan Agent

- The `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField` block reuses the
  parent `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint`'s Hartshorne II.6 verbatim
  source quote (the
  `\mathscr L(D)`-as-subsheaf-of-`\mathscr K` definition). This is
  legitimate: the inclusion $\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
  \mathcal K_C$ is **directly part of** Hartshorne's definition of
  $\mathscr L(D)$ (the quote literally says
  "We define a subsheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total
  quotient rings $\mathscr K$"), so the taking-global-sections of
  this inclusion produces $\iota$ without requiring a fresh source.
  Both citation blocks now share the same verbatim text. No
  duplication concern.
- The `% NOTE: ...` comment on `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` points
  at `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`, which is in `analogies/` (not
  `references/`). The "verbatim from `references/...`" rule does
  NOT apply to `% NOTE: ...` comments (those are project-internal
  routing pointers, not external citation blocks), so this is fine.
- `RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex` now sits at 595 lines (was
  541); still under any reasonable split threshold. No
  reorganisation needed.

## Strategy-modifying findings

None. The two chapter edits are direct, mechanical consequences of:

  1. The iter-181 Lane I signature refinement landed in
     `RationalCurveIso.lean` (`Module.finrank ... = 1` replacing the
     `Nonempty (... ≃+* ...)` placeholder), per the
     `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2
     `DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY` verdict.
  2. The iter-182 plan-phase Pin 2 refactor (refactor slug
     `pin2-sig-strengthen`) refactoring the
     `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` signature to bind
     `D = poleDivisor φ` and `deg D = finrank K(C)/K(P¹)`,
     introducing the typed-sorry def `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor`.
  3. The iter-181 `lean-vs-blueprint-checker iter181-ocofp` minor
     finding recommending a blueprint pin for the
     `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField` typed-sorry def on
     `OCofP.lean:154`.

No strategic ambiguity surfaced. No STRATEGY.md update required.
